(1550 [AM](/wiki/AM_broadcasting "AM broadcasting")) is a [radio station](/wiki/Radio_station "Radio station") based in [Madison, Wisconsin](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin") and broadcasting a [classic country](/wiki/Classic_country "Classic country") format. The station is owned by [Mid-West Family Broadcasting](/wiki/Mid-West_Family_Broadcasting "Mid-West Family Broadcasting").

Radio station in Madison, Wisconsin, United States

**WHIT** (1550 [AM](/wiki/AM_broadcasting "AM broadcasting")) is a [radio station](/wiki/Radio_station "Radio station") based in [Madison, Wisconsin](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin") and broadcasting a [classic country](/wiki/Classic_country "Classic country") format. The station is owned by [Mid-West Family Broadcasting](/wiki/Mid-West_Family_Broadcasting "Mid-West Family Broadcasting").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WHIT&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

WHIT began broadcasting in 1948, licensed to Madison and operating with a daytime power of 5,000 watts.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In its early decades, the station was known for its "Full Service" and "Middle of the Road" (MOR) music approach, often branding itself as a station for "Good Music" to distinguish it from the emerging rock and roll stations in the market.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

By the 1970s, WHIT transitioned to a country music format, which remained its primary identity for nearly two decades.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The station was a prominent local affiliate for Nashville-based programming and live country music news.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In the mid-1990s, the station experimented with various formats, including oldies and adult standards, as listener habits shifted toward the FM dial.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

By the late 1990s/early 2000s, the station was airing an all-sports format known as "The Team."

In 2001, the station turned to an [adult standards](/wiki/Adult_standards "Adult standards") format as WTUX ("The Tux"), affiliated with the [Music of Your Life](/wiki/Music_of_Your_Life "Music of Your Life"), [Jones Standards](/wiki/Jones_Standards "Jones Standards") and [Dial Global](/wiki/Dial_Global "Dial Global") [America's Best Music](/wiki/America%27s_Best_Music "America's Best Music") networks. On January 19, 2009, the station dropped adult standards and switched back to an automated oldies format, readopting the WHIT legacy [call sign](/wiki/Call_sign "Call sign") and focusing on music from the 1950s and 1960s. (As part of WHIT's flip, another Mid-West Family station in Michigan that had the WHIT call sign was assigned the [WSCY](/wiki/WCSY_(AM) "WCSY (AM)") calls to match its FM simulcast station.) WHIT played oldies on an automated basis until joining the nationally distributed [True Oldies Channel](/wiki/The_True_Oldies_Channel "The True Oldies Channel") on November 14, 2011.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

On December 6, 2013, WHIT changed their format from oldies to classic country, branded as "Hank on AM 1550".[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On October 8, 2020, WHIT relaunched as "97.7 The Farm", retaining the classic country format, but with enhanced farm news coverage from Pam Jahnke’s "Mid-West Farm Report" daily from 5–6am. Jahnke and Josh Scramlin have additional news updates and features all day on The Farm.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
